data.title: AWS Puts Agent-Focused Platform Center Stage
data.description:
AI-powered agents have unquestionably become the hot topic for all the largest tech vendors, as Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN, TSX:AMZN:CA) latest event in New York City once again demonstrated. However, as awareness of the complexities of creating and widely deploying agents has grown, so too has the number of questions regarding how to make that happen.

At AWS Summit, Amazon’s cloud computing division addressed those challenges head-on with a range of offerings covering development and deployment tools for agents, as well as a new marketplace from which customers can purchase finished agent products. In sum, it represented a comprehensive vision that AWS hopes will position it as a compelling platform for companies that want to start integrating agents into their environments, regardless of their level of IT sophistication.

One of the key offerings AWS unveiled was Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, which provides a suite of services for building custom agents that can leverage a wide range of different models and agent-building platforms, including open-source options such as Langchain, CrewAI and LlamaIndex. On top of that, AWS introduced its own new open-source agent-building tool it calls Strands Agents. As AWS VP Swami Sivasubramanian mentioned in his keynote, agents aren’t just the hot new AI buzzword, they represent a fundamental rethinking and evolution of how software applications are built, distributed, and used. That, in turn, makes tools like Strands Agents very important for the future of software and the platforms on which they run.

The key elements available through Bedrock AgentCore include a run-time environment for agents that integrates a range of security, reliability, and scaling capabilities. One of the big questions and concerns regarding agents is the potential impact—both positive and negative—that they can quickly have in a given environment because of their autonomous capabilities. To help build trust around agents, Amazon has integrated a series of guardrails, and it leverages its 10+-year old Lambda serverless architecture in which agents can be run. In addition, the company has integrated identity verification capabilities via AgentCore Identity, including the ability to tap into popular commercial and open-source authentication platforms such as OAuth. On top of that, one of the AgentCore services is an observability tool called AgentCore Observability that allows organizations to track how agents are performing, to see the type of data they’re accessing, and more.

Speaking of data, one of the key requirements for optimizing agents’ performance is to provide access to the specific data sets that need to be leveraged to perform certain workflows. AgentCore Gateway provides tools to access various APIs and other tools to make these connections possible. In addition, Amazon is supporting both the MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) standards in a significant way via Gateway, giving companies more flexibility in the range of data sources, applications, and services to which they can connect.

Another important expectation for agents is the ability to build upon previous interactions through access to both short-term and long-term memory. The AgentCore Memory services makes the process of creating these types of often complicated memory structures much easier, allowing developers to create more compelling (and ultimately more successful) agentic experiences. Finally, for companies to monitor how the specific code of a given agent is processing data, Amazon has also created a service it’s calling AgentCore Code Interpreter that can be used to visualize more complex data interactions with certain types of agents.

In addition to the agent-building capabilities, AWS also announced several other key tools to help with the creation and fine tuning of AI models that can be used to drive these agentic applications. For the company’s family of Nova foundation models, Amazon announced the release of Nova Act, which can be used to drive autonomous actions within browsers. Given how critical browser-based applications and searches are to so many workflows, these capabilities will likely be used extensively for agentic AI applications. In fact, the AgentCore Browser service provides this specific capability within the AgentCore platform. Plus, because AgentCore supports the ability to use multiple models for different parts of an agentic workflow, organizations can leverage Nova Act for certain actions while using other LLMs for reasoning-based actions, for example.

For companies working to train or fine-tune their models, there were two important announcements at Summit. First, the company unveiled new capabilities for its SageMaker AI model-building tool that allows organizations to more easily customize Nova models by linking to their own data sources. Second, the company revealed a new type of S3 storage service called Amazon S3 Vectors that is specifically optimized for cost-effectively storing large amounts of the vector data that’s typically used for model training and fine tuning. Together, these two offerings should make it easier for enterprises to build more customized and effective models. It’s also worth noting that these capabilities can be used independently of any agentic AI work but also fit nicely into the overall custom agent development process.

For companies that would prefer to buy instead of build when it comes to agents, AWS also launched a new marketplace for commercially available agents. At launch, the marketplace already includes over 800 options ranging from general-purpose tools to industry and workflow specific options, highlighting how quickly this market is evolving. To put that in perspective, in conversations with an AWS Marketplace exec, he mentioned that the company’s SaaS-based Marketplace launched with only about 50 partners. The goal with this new Agent Marketplace is to provide a storefront for software partners as well as enable enterprises to easily find solutions to fit their specific needs.

Taken together, the announcements from AWS Summit reflect a very focused effort by the company to position itself at the cutting edge of the hottest new technologies. As with many big tech announcements, much of the news comes before many big real-world deployments of agentic AI have started to happen. But, as companies start to experiment with these technologies, it’s essential to provide a set of tools with which they can begin these efforts. Plus, by creating the Agent Marketplace, AWS is also giving companies an easy way to dip their toes into the agentic world. Similar to GenAI overall, we’re still very early in the agentic AI era, but as these latest announcements confirm, the pace of development continues to be blistering.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: DWX- Is It Better Than VYMI?
data.description:
A Change In Script For DWX

The SPDR S&P International Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:DWX) is a 17-year old product (it was set-up in February 2008). While it has been a long-standing source of price destruction (down by -43% since its inception), it appears to have regained its mojo in 2025.

To elaborate, during the first six-and-a-half months of 2025 alone, DWX has surged by almost 20%, which is almost twice the returns of global stocks, and roughly three times the returns of US stocks.

If DWX’s strong alpha this year has sufficiently piqued your interest in this product, and you’re looking to better understand this product and its chief competitor, this article is for you.

What Does DWX ETF Do?

DWX is a passively managed ETF, which seeks to cover the S&P International Dividend Opportunities Index; the latter essentially covers 100 high-yielding stocks from ~23 countries across the globe (excluding the US).

DWX covers its tracking index by way of sampling, which means it won’t necessarily purchase all the stocks in the index and in the same proportion, but rather just a subset of stocks that have a similar risk and return profile to the index. Typically, ETFs which resort to sampling (rather than replication) tend to produce high tracking errors of double-digit percentages, but that is not the case with DWX, although over the past year, the degree of tracking error has ramped up beyond what most ETFs see.

It’s fair to say that DWX is more of a non-US developed market ETF as it ensures that emerging market [EM] exposure is capped at just 15% of the total portfolio, while it also does not consider any China A shares. EM exposure of DWX is currently also largely coming from the likes of Taiwan and South Korea, both of which are not widely defined as EMs.

How Is DWX’s Tracking Index Built?

The S&P International Dividend Opportunities Index looks for potential high yielding stocks from the S&P Global ex-U.S. BMI Index.

In the first step, China A shares are weeded out. Micro-caps also won’t be considered, as they maintain a minimum market-cap threshold of $500M. Also, stocks with daily traded value of less than $5M won’t be considered.

The next stage is when one gets to ascertain the quality of the high-yielders as quite a few supplementary screeners are put to good use. Firstly, we appreciate that DWX doesn’t pursue businesses that pay out dividends without generating any profits (there’s a requirement of positive reported EPS over the last twelve months). Then these businesses also need to generate ‘Funds from operations’ over ‘dividends’ of 1x (a measure of dividend coverage), dividend growth over three years (although declines are permitted so long as it is less than 5%). And finally, these stocks also need to have a dividend yield that is higher than the median dividend yield of the remaining universe (by remaining universe we mean stocks from the S&P Global ex-U.S. BMI Index universe, which have also cleared the $500m market-cap and $5m daily trading barrier).

All in all, we appreciate that DWX has not blindly just picked up the highest yielding 100 stocks from across the world, but has also put in place these investability and sustainability screeners.

In the next stage, DWX then attempts to pick stocks on the basis of a risk-adjusted yield metric, which helps focus on high yielders that don’t witness a great deal of variability in their yields. To be more specific, the dividend yield of a certain stock is divided by the 36-month volatility of the monthly dividend yield. Eventually the chosen stocks are weighted on the basis of the trailing twelve-month dividend yield.

Note that the index gets rebalanced twice a year, and it also takes steps to cap sector and country weights at 25%, and single stock weight at 3% (also as mentioned in the previous section of this article, there is an EM cap of 15%).

Is DWX Better Than VYMI?

Those who’d like to pursue high-yielding international equities via the passive ETF route, are typically directed towards the Vanguard International High Dividend Yield ETF (VYMI) which tracks the FTSE All-World ex US High Dividend Yield Index. VYMI’s popularity can be gleaned by its gargantuan AUM of close to $12B (whereas DWX’s AUM is far lower at less than $0.5B), which it has managed, despite only being around for less than 10 years. Since VYMI attracts most of the ETF flows within the international high yielding space, it would make more sense to measure DWX against this juggernaut, rather than reviewing DWX in isolation.

Unlike DWX, VYMI’s tracking index doesn’t impose too many screeners in the selection process. It essentially looks for its stocks from the FTSE All-World Index which covers both domestic and EM candidates, although REITs are not considered. VYMI doesn’t seek to maintain any restrictions on EMs unlike DWX (VYMI’s current EM exposure is at 22%). While VYM doesn’t really incorporate any clear dividend sustainability screeners, it does not consider stocks that are forecasted to pay zero dividends over the next 12 months. Unlike DWX’s tracking index which follows quite a sophisticated methodology, the goal here is basically to just pick the top 50% of high yielders (based on the forward yield) from the FTSE All-World Index. As a result, note that VYMI is also much larger in scope, covering over 1650 stocks (whereas DWX just covers 100 stocks). DWX also tilts more towards mid-caps, whereas VYMI is more of a giant-cap play. So what are the end results?

Well, as you can see from the image below, both on a price basis, as well as a total return basis (which incorporates dividends), it is VYMI which comes out on top.

Having said that, prima facie, it looks like DWX has the lower risk profile of the two, with a rolling volatility profile that is 140bps lower.

But does that really leave an inimical mark when it comes to the risk-adjusted return track record? Well for that, one may consider looking at the Sharpe ratio (it measures the excess returns of an ETF over the risk-free rate, relative to the standard deviation of the ETF) of these two products across different periods. Be it over the past five years, three years or one year, DWX has consistently lagged its larger peer on the Sharpe ratio front.

VYMI also comes across as the preferable bet for value conscious investors, as it is priced at a P/E discount of -27% to DWX, yet also offers slightly better long-term earnings growth potential.

Even if one wants to gauge the structural qualities of both these products, we feel VYMI is the better product. Firstly, it is more cost accessible, coming in at an expense ratio of just 0.17%, while DWX is 2.65x higher. Because DWX takes great efforts to maintain various caps, it also resorts to a great deal of churn (6 out of 10 stocks get turned over every year) whereas VYMI is a lot more stable, witnessing annual churn of a little over 11%. Then, short-term market players who place a lot of emphasis on getting good fills won’t be troubled by pursuing VYMI, given its relatively tight spreads of 0.04% (DWX’s spreads are 3x more). Finally also note that while both products distribute quarterly, VYMI has grown its dividends at a faster pace, and currently offers a better yield than DWX.

Closing Thoughts

As implied in the previous section, it is rather clear that DWX comes across as an inferior ETF for those looking for exposure to high-yielding international equities. We would prefer to play this theme via VYMI.

data.title: LQD: Slim Spreads And Rising Risks
data.description:
The margin of safety is always dependent on the price paid. It will be large at one price, small at some higher price, nonexistent at some still higher price.

- Benjamin Graham

Investment-grade bonds, just like equities, require an adequate margin of safety to truly protect investors in uncertain times. At the moment, we do not see much, if any, margin of safety with investment grade corporate bonds, for which we can use a popular ETF such as the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:LQD) as a proxy.

As Benjamin Graham's student, Warren Buffett once said, "the three most important words in investing are margin of safety". For corporate bonds that margin of safety comes in the form of real yield, and spread over Treasuries with similar maturities. This interest rate spread is necessary to compensate for potential defaults and the additional risk investors are taking by lending to more risky entities. Corporate bonds also have a higher correlation to the stock market, making them less useful for diversification purposes, another reason they should provide more yield.

There are already signs of potential trouble ahead, commercial bank loan delinquencies have been trending higher for several quarters, several companies now have to refinance cheap bonds issued during the near-zero interest rate environment at much higher rates, and recently some high-profile companies have been downgraded by credit agencies. To give some examples, Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) was downgraded by Moody's (MCO) last June as the company announced plans to split its business, with its senior unsecured debt now at Ba1 making them junk bonds. Another example is Ford Motor Company (F), which is at risk of having its credit cut to junk after S&P Global (SPGI) affirmed the company's 'BBB-' rating, but revised the outlook to negative in February as a result of weaker-than-expected profitability and tariffs risks. This makes Ford Motor a potential fallen angel, and one of the largest by the amount of debt. Both these companies are currently found in LQD's holdings.

Portfolio Characteristics

There are a few things to like about LQD, including a relatively low expense ratio of 0.14%, significant diversification with almost three thousand holdings, and positive and relatively high convexity compared to a bond ETF like the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG). All else equal, positive convexity is better as it means larger price increases when yields fall compared to price declines when yields increase. The reason ETFs like AGG have lower convexity is that they tend to include callable bonds and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), which often exhibit negative convexity given the borrower's option to call or prepay the bonds. Less appealing is the relatively high effective duration of 7.98 years, which is close to the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), which has an effective duration of 7 years and we will use for comparison.

We are also not impressed with the average credit quality, with roughly 43% of the ETF's value in BBB rated debt. Some of these companies risk becoming fallen angels, no longer being considered investment grade, and are often sold at meaningful discounts after the companies are stripped of their investment-grade credit rating. In general, being a forced seller leads to selling at a discount to fair value. On the other side of this trade are ETFs like the iShares Fallen Angels USD Bond ETF (FALN) and the VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL), which have tended to outperform as they purchase at a discount.

Despite the wide diversification, there is a relatively high concentration to the banking sector, with roughly 23% of market value invested in this category, followed by consumer non-cyclical at over 17% of total market value. The banking exposure is particularly concerning, as financial institutions are usually highly leveraged, and one important failure can cascade through the industry, as we can remember from the global financial crisis.

Slim Spreads

Still, these issues might not be that important if the ETF were trading with a significant valuation margin of safety. However, the spread between LQD's dividend yield, and a Treasury Bond ETF with a similar duration, such as IEF, is near the lowest in a decade. Investors are getting very little in terms of additional yield to compensate for the credit risk, as well as the higher correlation that corporate bonds have with equity markets.

Credit Defaults

We already mentioned some recent notable downgrades, but looking at the overall picture it is clear that credit metrics have been deteriorating for some time. For example, the commercial banks delinquency rate on loans has been on an uptrend over the past couple of years, and it is now close to the ten-year average. In their second-quarter of 2025 investment-grade credit check report, S&P Global noted that potential fallen angels outweigh potential rising stars, or companies that could move from junk to investment grade, by over 70%. This is a clear sign that credit quality momentum is in decline.

Tariffs and Recession Risks

While the odds of a recession have recently receded, they remain high by historical standards. Most economists also agree that a worst-case scenario trade war would increase the odds of a recession significantly. Tariffs would certainly impact some industries and companies directly, but others could be affected indirectly as well as a result of the general economic slowdown. At the same time, consumer sentiment is close to a multi-decade low, although it has slightly rebounded recently.

Alternatives Comparison

While past performance is no guarantee of future returns, there are structural reasons why ETFs like FALN and ANGL are in a good position to outperform LQD over the long-term. These ETFs aim to purchase companies that recently lost their investment-grade credit rating, but are usually still far from default. With many investors forced to sell these bonds, their prices tend to be overly punished beyond what the downgrade would justify. The JNK ETF has also outperformed LQD, but we are more concerned about its credit quality and do not see a structural tailwind. In the case of the IEF ETF, total return has been extremely low as the ETF digests the impact of bonds bought when Treasuries were yielding near zero interest. Still, IEF could outperform in a rush to safe havens and has on average a lower correlation to equity markets.

Valuation

The trailing-twelve-months dividend yield is close to the ten-year average, the thirty day SEC yield is 5.2%, and the average yield to maturity is 5.3%. The problem is that Treasury yields have significantly increased, with IEF's dividend yield ~120 bps above its ten-year average. LQD's yield appears reasonable, until it is compared to safer alternatives.

Risks

While high-grade corporate bonds have a reputation as relatively stable investments, they can sometimes hide significant risks, especially when markets become complacent. Inflation remains elevated, interest rates uncertain, and credit risk is rapidly deteriorating, as seen by some notable credit downgrades in recent months. The key risk with LQD, though, is that its interest spread over Treasuries has compressed, which is particularly concerning at a time of economic uncertainty and deteriorating credit risk.

Conclusion

The interest rate spread of high-grade corporate bonds with equivalent maturity Treasury bonds has compressed significantly, reducing the margin of safety in case of defaults and delinquencies. As inflation remains elevated, interest rates uncertain, and credit risks escalate, investors holding bond ETFs like LQD might be unknowingly exposed to greater downside potential than anticipated.

When the yield premium shrinks too low, even wide diversification won’t fully shield investors from the effects of tariff disruptions or an economic downturn. For investors willing to consider higher risk alternatives we believe fallen angel ETFs have a structural advantage, and offer a more attractive yield. Alternatively, for investors that prefer taking less risk, an intermediate Treasury bond ETF like IEF might be worth considering.

data.title: BCV: An Improving Convertible Bond Fund, Should Be Better Than Traditional Bonds
data.description:
The Bancroft Fund (NYSE:BCV) is a somewhat underfollowed convertible bond fund managed by Gabelli Funds. It is actually somewhat surprising that this fund is not more widely followed, given the general reputation and popularity that Gabelli tends to enjoy. As of right now, there are only 1,030 investors following this particular fund on Seeking Alpha:

It is possible, though, that this is partly due to the fund’s somewhat disappointing performance in the past few years. The Bancroft Fund has only managed to deliver a 35.89% total return over the three-year period, trailing the S&P 500 Index (SP500TR) by a fairly significant amount over the same period:

The fund’s long-term performance is not really much better, as the fund has only managed to deliver a 120.49% total return over the past ten years. This is less than half of what the total return S&P 500 Index delivered:

However, we can see from the chart above that this fund was actually doing fairly well until late 2021. As some investors may remember, there was something of a convertible bond bubble in 2021 as low interest rates allowed some companies to issue convertible bonds with a 0% interest rate and the promise that investors would receive their compensation whenever they converted the bond into the equity of the issuing company.

As Lewis Enterprises explains in a blog post, convertibles of this nature are not really much more than an option, due to the simple fact that if the stock of the issuing company never reaches the conversion price, then the investor’s return is essentially zero in nominal terms and negative when inflation is considered. These bonds did fairly well in the pandemic when the time value of money was basically zero (the thirty-year U.S. Treasury had a yield under 1%) and the markets were constantly moving upward (which made investors confident that the bond would pay off through the conversion feature), but they did not perform nearly as well when interest rates rose and investors could actually earn respectable yields from traditional bonds.

We still occasionally see companies manage to issue 0% convertible bonds today, such as Strategy’s (MSTR) issuance of 0% convertible bonds earlier this year, but these are typically unicorn companies that investors think will be worth enormous amounts of money in the near future. However, most companies are not able to make such issuances, and overall, investors are not as willing to purchase 0% convertible bonds as they were during the pandemic era. Thus, the convertible bond bubble burst once interest rates started rising, and the Bancroft Fund, along with most convertible bond funds, took fairly significant losses.

This does not, however, mean that convertible bonds or convertible bond funds are a bad investment today. In fact, a very good case can be made for income investors purchasing them since they provide bond-like yields, but the conversion feature provides equity-like protection against inflation. In fact, convertible bonds have done a much better job at protecting investors against inflation than either investment-grade bonds (AGG) or junk bonds (JNK) over the past five years:

This chart shows the total real returns provided by convertible bonds (ICVT), investment-grade bonds, and junk bonds since July 17, 2020, assuming that investors reinvested all distributions paid out by the index funds tracking them into additional shares. The figures shown above are also adjusted for inflation using the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. As we can see, the only one of the three indices that managed to beat inflation over that period was the one tracking convertible bonds. This proves that convertible bonds have historically done a better job at maintaining their value in the face of inflation than have traditional bonds and therefore proves my point.

There are reasons that investors should continue to protect themselves against inflation today, despite the recent claims of policymakers that inflation has finally been “beaten.” The most recent inflation report is showing signs that inflation may be reaccelerating, and the long-term budget outlook for the United States still effectively guarantees that the low-inflation environment that the United States enjoyed for the past four decades is likely over. Thus, investors would be wise to hold securities that protect their purchasing power, especially if they need the income from their portfolios to pay their bills. Convertible securities do a much better job of this than traditional bonds, so this suggests that a convertible bond fund such as the Bancroft Fund might well deserve a look.

Peer Distribution Comparison

As of the time of writing, the Bancroft Fund sports a 6.65% yield. While this is not particularly attractive compared to the yields offered by some closed-end funds ("CEFs") investing in traditional bonds, it does still match or exceed the yields of most fixed-income index funds. We can see that here:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.86%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.59%

Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Cash Pay Bond > $250 million Index

2.06%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

6.57%

As we can see, the Bancroft Fund has a higher yield than any of the bond or preferred stock indices. When we also consider that it should offer superior protection against inflation than all except for the convertible bond index, we can see that this fund may be a better investment going forward than any of the indices above. However, it is worth noting that this fund underperformed all except for the investment-grade bond index over the past five years:

As we can immediately see, the Bancroft Fund underperformed both the convertible bond index and the junk bond index over the period. The biggest reason for this is the very steep decline that the fund had back in 2022 when the convertible bond bubble burst. This is the unfortunate problem with leverage, as it amplifies losses and makes it very difficult for a fund to claw back from. If we look at just this fund’s year-to-date performance, it did much better:

The fund’s past performance may still be somewhat concerning for potential investors, but it is worth noting that only the fund’s future performance matters for anyone buying today. After all, today’s buyer will not be affected by yesterday’s price decline.

The yield of the Bancroft Fund is reasonably attractive when compared to various indices, but it is not especially high when compared to other convertible bond closed-end funds. We can see that in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Bancroft Fund

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund (NCV)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Convertibles

The only fund in the peer group that does not have a substantially higher yield than the Bancroft Fund is the Ellsworth and Income Fund. The Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund is also managed by Gabelli Funds, so it has a very similar portfolio and management style. As such, it makes sense that the Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund would have a similar yield.

Unfortunately, the low yield of the Bancroft Fund compared to its peers works against it when it comes to the provision of total returns. Over the past five years, this has been the worst-performing fund shown in the table above in this respect:

As we can see, all of the funds shown above provided their investors with better total returns, including the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund, which widely attracts criticism from investors due to its very steep decline following the bursting of the convertible bond bubble a few years ago. As a general rule, the higher a fund’s total return, the better it does at protecting investors against inflation over the same period. Thus, if we are looking for inflation protection, this fund appears to be somewhat disappointing when compared to its peers.

Naturally, though, past performance is no guarantee of future results, so let us have a look at this fund’s portfolio and overall positioning today in order to make an informed judgment about how it might perform going forward.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Bancroft Fund was on November 14, 2024. At the time, I thought that this fund was decent but did not really offer any notable advantages over its peers. However, this fund has actually performed fairly well since that time, as it was the second-best performing fund in terms of total return:

This does not appear to be a case in which the fund’s share price significantly outperformed the underlying portfolio, as we have seen with several other funds in recent months. In fact, the fund’s net asset value is up 8.27% compared to only a 7.73% gain for the fund’s share price:

The only fund that delivered a better performance over the period was the Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund, which provided its investors with a 14.64% total return over the period. This strong performance might have something to do with a few of the fund’s holdings. The fund’s website provides this list of the fund’s largest positions as of July 10, 2025:

We see a few companies here that have delivered very impressive returns over the past few months. In particular, MP Materials (MP) is up a whopping 255.13% year-to-date:

MP Materials made the news last week when the Pentagon announced that it would be buying $400 million of convertible preferred shares, along with a warrant that allows the Pentagon to purchase additional shares of common stock in the company. If the U.S. military should exercise the conversion feature on the convertible preferreds and purchase common stock under the warrants, then it will end up owning 15% of the company. This deal reminds me a lot of the policies that many European and Asian countries have with respect to some of their companies.

It is not uncommon for a national government to take ownership positions in companies that its leadership deems vital to the public interest. For example, the Norwegian government has a massive ownership stake in Equinor (EQNR), the French government was once one of the largest shareholders in TotalEnergies (TOT), and the Russian Federation owns a significant proportion of the outstanding common stock of Gazprom.

The United States government, on the other hand, has historically been reluctant to take positions in American companies, but this investment in MP Materials may be a sign that this is changing. The current leadership of the United States has made it clear that it sees the development of domestic rare earth mining as a key national objective, and MP Materials is one of the few domestic companies that is focused on the development of such resources. The fund’s most recent semi-annual report states that the fund invested in MP Materials prior to March 2025, so it had a position prior to the massive price surge. As such, it seems likely that the fund’s position in MP Materials was one of the positive drivers of its recent performance.

We also see that the Bancroft Fund has a position in Uber Technologies (UBER), which has also delivered very impressive performance year-to-date:

As we can see, Uber Technologies is up an impressive 50.55% year-to-date. This has been largely driven by the much-awaited “robotaxis” finally making an appearance in several U.S. cities. As Zero Hedge reported this morning:

America’s robotaxi race is entering a new phase, inching closer and closer to a hyperscaling inflection point. With Waymo rapidly expanding across U.S. cities and Tesla’s robotaxi fleet launching in Austin, Texas, this June, the momentum is building. Now, a new alliance emerges--Uber Technologies, EV maker Lucid Group, and autonomous driving startup Nuro--is preparing to enter the robotaxi market with a next generation premium global robotaxi program slated to launch in a major U.S. city in the second half of 2026.

Investors in Uber Technologies have long believed that Uber would be a participant in the emerging driverless vehicle sector, and as companies such as Waymo and Tesla (TSLA) actually began to deploy this technology, many market participants believed that Uber would not be too far behind. We now have confirmation that the company is planning to deploy this technology, the expectations of which were a significant factor in driving the firm’s stock price higher this year. This naturally handed some gains to the Bancroft Fund as well since a rising stock price causes the price of the fund’s convertible bonds to rise. It is unclear how much of a contribution Uber Technologies actually made to the fund’s outperformance compared to its peers, though, since many convertible bond funds hold Uber Technologies’ convertible bonds. We have seen this in various previous articles on other convertible bond funds.

In short, we can see that this fund appears to have benefited from the very strong performance of a few of its portfolio holdings over the past several months. The fund has outperformed most of its peers since mid-November, and it appears to have fully covered all of the distributions that it has paid out since that time. Unfortunately, this has also resulted in the fund having a fairly low yield when compared to other convertible bond funds.

About The Fund

The website for the Bancroft Fund (linked earlier) states that the fund has the primary objective of providing income, although it notes that it also expects to get some capital gains from the convertible securities in the portfolio. This actually makes a lot of sense for a convertible bond fund, as this is the way that convertible bonds provide their investment returns. At their core, these securities are bonds as they represent a loan to the issuer that the issuer then repays with interest. Bonds themselves provide no net capital gains over their lifetimes, as the initial payment and payment at maturity are the same nominal amount. However, the conversion feature that convertible bonds have acts as an option, and the price of options rises when the share price of the linked company rises.

It works the same way with convertible bonds, as the conversion feature becomes more valuable as the stock price of the issuing company rises. The conversion feature, if exercised, can actually result in the investor getting more than they originally lent to the company, so convertible bonds can deliver capital gains over their lifetimes.

The fund’s website states that the Bancroft Fund will normally have at least 65% of its assets invested in convertible securities. The fund’s semi-annual report (linked earlier) states that the fund had the following asset allocation as of March 31, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Total Assets

Convertible Corporate Bonds

81.6%

Convertible Preferred Stocks

0.4%

Mandatory Convertible Securities

13.0%

Common Stocks

0.0%

U.S. Treasury Bills

5.0%

As we immediately see, the Bancroft Fund appears to be fulfilling its mandate to have at least 65% of its assets invested in convertible securities. In fact, it is well above the 65% level that its website states it will typically have. As regular readers will likely guess, I rather like the allocation that we see here. It is certainly better than something like the Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (CHI), which has a significant proportion of its portfolio invested in traditional debt securities. The website for that fund provides this allocation:

Asset Type

% of Total Assets

Convertibles

66.67%

Corporation Bonds

24.15%

Bank Loans

4.85%

Cash and Receivables/Payables

3.54%

Common Stock

0.57%

Preferred Stock

0.08%

Options

0.07%

It is very difficult to like any traditional bonds at all right now due to their lack of protection against a currency devaluation. As international investor Nick Giambruno points out in an article on Doug Casey’s International Man, the long-term average growth of the U.S. money supply is around 7% annually. It has actually been less than that over the past five years:

As of May 2025, the M2 money supply in the United States is $21.942 trillion, up from $17.8534 trillion in May 2020. That is only a 22.90% increase over five years, which works out to about 4.59% per year. However, it is reasonable to expect that this pace will increase going forward as U.S. deficits grow and the Federal Reserve tries to hold interest rates down. When we consider this, it makes it very hard to believe that most bonds will be able to hold their value unless the United States delivers much higher gross domestic product growth than it has managed at any point in the past fifty years.

As we have already seen, even junk bonds only barely managed to deliver a positive real return over the past decade (and that is assuming that the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers). Investment-grade bonds failed completely as a store of value. As a country’s debt level rises, economic growth declines, and the simple fact that the United States is still projecting to run a deficit means that it will get ever more difficult for the country to continue to grow its economy. If the M2 money supply grows faster than the real economy, it represents currency debasement.

David Stockman, the director of the Office of Management and Budget during the Reagan Administration, has outright stated that there is no possible way that the United States can grow its way out of the deficits and debt currently in the economy and the projected deficits going forward. Thus, it is very difficult to believe that bonds will be able to hold their value against currency debasement unless interest rates increase substantially from today’s levels (and the Federal Reserve will almost certainly intervene in the bond market if that occurs). Thus, it is hard to make a case for any traditional bonds at all.

Convertible bonds, fortunately, do not have this problem as they have the conversion feature that links them to equity prices, and equities can protect their owners against currency debasement. I have explained this in the past. Thus, I am much more comfortable with a fund that is entirely invested in convertibles as opposed to one that has a mixture of convertible bonds and traditional bonds, at least over any sort of extended period. The Bancroft Fund thus has the superior asset allocation and portfolio composition out of its peers that include a mixture of both convertible bonds and traditional debt securities.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Bancroft Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a quarterly distribution of $0.3200 per share ($1.28 per share annually). This gives the fund a 6.65% yield at the current share price, which is not particularly attractive when compared to the yield offered by most other convertible bond closed-end funds.

This fund has been fairly consistent with its distribution for a while now, although it did vary somewhat back around the time of the COVID-19 pandemic:

Several of the distributions that are shown above are actually special distributions that this fund made in 2020 and 2021 due to the large gains that it was getting during the pandemic-era convertible bond bubble. However, the $0.2900 distributions that the fund made in June and September of 2020 were regular distributions. Thus, the fund has been raising its regular distribution over the past few years. The fact, though, that the Bancroft Fund pays a quarterly as opposed to a monthly distribution might reduce its appeal in the minds of some investors. This makes sense as most bills are due monthly, as opposed to quarterly.

In addition, quarterly distributions are not quite as good as monthly distributions for compounding purposes, so they slightly lower the total returns that investors can expect from this fund compared to what it would deliver with a $0.1067 per share monthly distribution. After all, the monthly distribution lets an investor accumulate additional shares more rapidly than is the case with quarterly distributions and thus grow their income more quickly over time.

With that said, we should have a look at how well this fund is covering its distribution, since we do not want a distribution cut. For this purpose, we will look at the fund’s most recent semi-annual report, which corresponds to the six-month period that ended on March 31, 2025. A link to this report was provided earlier in this article, and it is newer than the reports that were available to us at the time of our last discussion on this fund, so it should work pretty well for the provision of an update.

For the six-month period that ended on March 31, 2025, the Bancroft Fund reported a net investment income of $1,610,012. That was not sufficient to cover the $3,740,154 that the fund paid out to its investors via distributions over the same period.

Fortunately, though, this fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on March 31, 2025, the Bancroft Fund reported net realized losses of $437,927, but these were offset by $3,919,683 in net unrealized gains. The fund had to pay out $769,121 to holders of its outstanding preferred stock, but it still managed to deliver a $4,322,647 increase in its net assets from its investment operations:

This was sufficient to cover the fund’s distributions, so overall its distribution should be reasonably safe. However, it is worth noting that this fund was only able to cover its distribution due to its large amount of unrealized gains. This is not exactly ideal, since unrealized gains can be erased in a market correction. However, it is difficult to see a potential catalyst on the horizon that could push down the price of convertible bonds in aggregate. Indeed, over the long term, equities will probably rise due to currency debasement. Thus, we probably do not need to worry too much about a distribution cut in the near future.

Valuation

Shares of the Bancroft Fund are currently trading at a 12.12% discount on net asset value. This is a better price than the 11.56% discount that the shares have had on average over the past month.

Historically, though, the Bancroft Fund has traded at a better price than it is today. Over the past three years, shares of the Bancroft Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 7.38% discount to a 17.32% discount on net asset value. The average price over the trailing three-year period has been a 13.45% discount on net asset value, which is a more attractive price than the fund has right now. Thus, this fund is currently at a fairly expensive price compared to its historical average valuation.

Here is how the price of this fund today compares to that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Bancroft Fund

(12.12%)

Advent Convertible and Income Fund

(3.82%)

Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund

3.52%

Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund

(10.37%)

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund

(9.79%)

This chart clearly shows that the current price of the Bancroft Fund compares very well to that of its peers. This is overall the cheapest fund shown in the chart.

The takeaway here is that this fund does not appear to be particularly expensive, and while it might be possible to get a better price by waiting for the discount to widen further, it is also hard to complain about the fund possessing a double-digit discount on net asset value.

Expenses

The fund’s semi-annual report states that the Bancroft Fund had a 1.33% expense ratio for the six-month period that ended on March 31, 2025. This represents an improvement over the 1.41% expense ratio that the fund reported for the full-year period that ended on September 30, 2024.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Bancroft Fund has been one of the better-performing convertible closed-end funds over the past several months. This is at least partly due to some very good investments that it made in MP Materials, Uber Technologies, and other companies. Despite this strong performance, this fund continues to trade at a double-digit discount to its net asset value, so it is not particularly expensive. The large discount might be due to the fund’s historical performance not being nearly as impressive as it has delivered in recent months.

It is uncertain whether or not the fund’s performance will stay strong going forward, or if it will decline back to levels comparable to its historical averages. Convertibles should prove to be a much better holding than any traditional fixed-income security going forward, however.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WesBanco: Acquisition Boosts Growth, But Asset Quality And Valuation Warrant Caution
data.description:
Introduction

WesBanco, Inc. (NASDAQ:WSBC) is a bank holding company of WesBanco Bank, a prominent US regional bank headquartered in West Virginia, focusing on providing banking services in the eastern US region. The company recently completed its acquisition of Premier Financial Corporation (PFC) in February 2025 which has aided its 1Q25 results. The tailwind for this acquisition should continue in the coming quarter in 2Q25 along with the underlying NIMs tailwind due to repricing trends. Moreover, the loan and deposit growth trend is also better than many regional bank peers. However, the recent asset quality deterioration has made me a little worried regarding the stock. While the management has mentioned that most of the deterioration was attributed to the acquisition, I believe one should wait for these numbers to settle. Moreover, the company’s valuations are not enticing on either of the valuation approaches based on PEG and yield. So I have a neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

Quarter aided by acquisition and positive underlying trend

Back in late April, WSBC came out with a mixed set of numbers, with its bottom line beating consensus estimates while its top line missed the estimates. While the numbers were somewhat muted as compared to estimates, when compared to the same quarter last year, the performance was quite good, with WSBC’s revenue growing 33.6% YoY to $193.2 million primarily due to Premier Financial Corp’s [PFC] acquisition. On a pro forma basis, the organic revenue grew by 6.9% YoY from $202.3 million to $247.6 million. On the bottom line front, the company reported a non-GAAP EPS of $0.66 per share, a substantial increase of 18% YoY even after accounting for dilution related to the PFC acquisition.

On a more granular basis, the company’s net interest income during the quarter grew by 39% YoY to $158.5 million primarily due to the acquisition but some part of this growth was also attributed to NIM’s expansion. The company has been reporting consistent increases in NIMs for the last few quarters on the back of an improved mix toward higher-yielding assets that came from the PFC acquisition and lower funding costs, primarily declining deposit rates.

Quarter

Net Interest Margin (NIM)

1Q25

3.35%

4Q24

3.03%

3Q24

2.95%

2Q24

2.95%

1Q24

2.92%

Source: Company filing, author

With respect to loan book growth, the company reported 57.3% growth on a YoY basis to $18.7 billion, again primarily driven by the acquisition impact. Excluding the acquisition impact, the growth should have been ~8% YoY, which is in itself quite good. The organic growth was well rooted across the banking segments but particularly strong in the commercial loan segment, which grew by ~10% YoY. The deposit's growth was also in line with the loan growth, growing 57.8% YoY including acquisition and 6.8% YoY excluding the benefit of acquisition.

Quarter Ending

Loan book YoY growth

1Q25

57.28%

4Q24

3Q24

10.00%

2Q24

10.00%

1Q24

9.00%

Source: Company filing, author.

With respect to asset quality, the company reported deterioration in the majority of the asset quality metrics. Starting with the NPA ratio, which increased from 0.22% in 4Q24 to 0.30% in 1Q25. While the ratio is below the industry-wide NPA levels, the increasing trend over the last two quarters is still worrying. To cope with the increasing NPA levels, the company has of late been ramping up its allowance for credit losses, which increased to 1.25% in 1Q25 from 1.1% in 4Q24. While some increase in the recent quarter is attributed to the PFC acquisition, there has also been the underlying asset quality stress within the commercial real estate portfolio of the company as well, which has been evident in the deteriorating NPA trend in 4Q24.

Going forward, the NIMs tailwind should continue as the full benefit from the acquisition-related higher-yielding assets shall flow in 2Q25 which should lead WSBC’s NIMs in the 3.5% range. With respect to the legacy NIM trajectory, the company should witness ~3-5 bps per quarter improvement even without Fed fund rate cuts, primarily driven by lower funding costs, especially on the certificates of deposit [CDs] front. Almost two-thirds of the $3 billion CD book is expected to reprice at a lower yield over the next six-month period with an average interest rate of 3.9% compared to current rates of ~3.5%. With respect to rate cut sensitivity, the company’s loan portfolio is fairly neutral to the rate sensitivity, leading it to remain largely unaffected by the cuts that are anticipated in 2025 and 2026.

With respect to loan book growth, the company anticipates mid-single-digit loan book growth, which is in line with other regional banks' guidance but what sets WSBC apart is their recent organic loan book growth, which is comparatively higher than other regional banks. Moreover, the PFC acquisition would also aid in growing into newer segments and areas. While there are instances where businesses are hesitant to commit to borrowing given the uncertain tariff backdrop, the momentum in loan growth as well as acquisition synergy benefits should partially negate this headwind. Moreover, potential future lower rates should further boost loan growth as it becomes cheaper to borrow.

With respect to asset quality, management is confident of the future trend in the asset quality benchmark, citing it to be better than the industry going forward, claiming the recent uptick in NPA is primarily due to the acquisition. While the company’s asset quality metrics are in quite a good zone, and their loan portfolio also has fewer than 4% of the loan book in the stressed office loan category, with the majority of it having a good LTV ratio, I still believe applying a little caution on this front would be wise, and waiting for the NPA to stabilize for at least one quarter could be a better decision.

Overall, WSBC has a good NIMs runway ahead with good loan book growth on the cards. While asset quality indicators have worsened recently, it is a little less worrisome given the one-time impact of the acquisition, but still caution should be applied.

Valuation and conclusion

WSBC is currently trading at a 9.6x non-GAAP forward P/E ratio, a notch lower than the sector median of 11x and its own 5-year average of 12.2x. While the company is forecasted to have quite good growth numbers going forward (~14% eps growth in 2026), its past 10 years of growth have been suboptimal, with the diluted normalized EPS in fact declining for this period. This could be due to the high payout ratio of WSBC. Even if we assume ROE (5-year average, 6%) to be equal to the growth figure, the company still yields a PEG ratio of 1.5x, which is decent but not decent enough for a buy rating.

From the book value perspective, the company still appears to be a little overvalued. The company is currently trading at ~0.8x forward price-to-book ratio, which is lower than the sector median of 1.16x and a notch higher than its own 5-year average of 0.78x. When the 0.8x P/B ratio is compared with the average ROE of 6%, we get a yield of ~7.5% (ROE/Price-to-Book). Again, the yield of 7.5% is decent but not decent enough for a buy rating.

In conclusion, while the company has good NIMs and loan growth prospects, its valuation does not seem favorable enough to dish out a buy rating even though surface-level valuation numbers look cheap. In addition, the asset quality trend is also a little worrying, which requires improvement before one could get in the stock. Given all this, I have a neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

Risk to watch out for

  • The primary risk, as mentioned before regarding WSBC is regarding the deteriorating asset quality. While it is nothing alarming right now, one needs to keep an eye on the asset quality indicators (NPA ratio and others).
  • Another risk that the company faces is regarding the tariff-related uncertainties. While the US has come up with trade deals with some trading partners, trade deals with many, including large partners, are still pending. The uncertainty regarding the tariffs creates an environment of ambiguity for businesses, leading them to risk off or deleverage rather than borrow to reduce risk for a potential future harsher macroeconomic environment. This could lead to lower loan growth than expected.

data.title: Farmland Partners: Not Enough Discount To Net Asset Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Farmland Partners Inc. (NYSE:FPI) has significantly improved its financial figures since it started the deleveraging process. The company has reduced the interest expenses, the revenues per acre have improved, and the value of its land is steadily rising.

While the company will keep unlocking shareholder value by selling its land holdings, the core business generates poor results.

Based on the most recent land sales, I believe Farmland Partners' land has a market price ~15% higher than the book value, and the company trades at a discount to its net asset value (NAV).

However, I do not find the 10% discount to NAV compelling enough to start a position in the company.

Farmland Partners Overview

Farmland Partners is a publicly traded REIT with a market cap of $510M that owns and manages over 139,000 acres of farmland across the U.S.

Most of the revenues are generated through fixed rents (~65%), but FPI also sells crops cultivated in the farms it directly operates, which are sensitive to food prices, underwrites insurances, makes loans to farmers, manages land, auctions, and brokerage fees.

Although the direct impact of food prices is limited to the variable rent agreements and the farms directly operated by FPI, over 60% of leases expire within three years, so food prices and farmers' profitability will impact rent prices.

One of the most positive aspects of FPI's business is the zero vacancy rates, which provide a reliable cash flow stream. However, despite the post-pandemic increase in food prices, Farmland Partners has delivered a poor performance since its IPO.

As shown in the chart below, the company's stock has moved sideways for years, and the $4.78 per share paid in dividends since 2014 represents a yield of less than 3% per year.

In my view, the cause of this mediocre performance has been the excessive leverage and the aggressive land acquisition strategy in its early days.

Deleveraging Process and Interest Expenses

During its first years as a publicly traded company, Farmland Partners went on a buying spree that substantially increased its leverage ratios.

Since 2013, the company multiplied by over 20x its land ownership, but on the peak of its debt levels (2017 to 2021), around 35% of its debt had variable interest rates.

As shown in the chart below, despite the increase in revenues and operating income before the pandemic, the higher interest expenses and the share dilution caused a decrease in EPS.

Since 2023, after the increase in interest rates, Farmland Partners started a deleveraging process that has reduced interest expenses without impacting much operating income, and allowed it to pay a special dividend of $1.15 per share in late 2024.

The company has reduced its land ownership by 44% since its 2022 peak, but long-term debt has declined by 65%. During Q1 2025, interest expenses decreased from $5M a year ago to $2.6M, so I believe the company is taking the right steps to unlock shareholder value.

While FPI hasn't generated much income from its business over the years, the recent land sales have resulted in significant capital gains, since the land is recorded in the balance sheet at a lower value compared to market prices, especially after the recent increase in farmland prices.

It is important to note that on top of the recent land price increases, the company owns land in some of the most expensive regions of the country, such as California, Iowa, and Illinois.

The management expects to reduce its exposure in California as soon as prices improve, so I expect FPI's financials to keep improving as the company deleverages.

California has a variety of challenges. With water being probably the first and biggest challenge, the second being overplanting of many of those crops around the world. And the third being sort of labor challenges as -- labor and regulatory challenges, particularly in the State of California.

Source: Paul Pittman (Executive Chairman), Q4 2024 Earnings Call.

Profitability of the Business

But apart from the proceeds from land sales, what is the real profitability of the business?

Last year, Farmland Partners generated $58M in revenues and $27M in operating income, but if we take into account the $18.8M in interest expenses, it only generated $8.3M in EBT, so let's take a look at the different segments of the business before getting into the value of its land and the discount to net asset value.

As mentioned before, rental income generates most of FPI's revenues, and given the recent sales of land and the limited room for further price hikes, I do not expect an increase in the upcoming quarters.

And so remember, we got 20% increases in a couple of years ago, 15% in a couple of years ago in a different year. So we've got some very, very strong rent increases. And now we're in a little more of a pause in terms of our ability to push rents.

Source: Paul Pittman (Executive Chairman), Q1 2025 Earnings Call.

Despite the 31% YoY decrease in rental income during the first quarter, the company received $1M from a solar panel project, and even though management has stated that they do not expect renewable energies to become a substantial percentage of revenues, it could improve the profitability per acre.

As shown in the chart below, the profitability per acre from rental income has increased in recent years, and the company has 3,800 acres (4% of total acreage) under option for solar development projects.

Note: Q1 2025 figures include rental revenues for the last twelve months.

While the renewable energy segment could improve profitability, I believe the company might benefit from exiting its directly operated land. Last year, FPI generated $1.1M in gross income from direct crop sales, but in 2023, it recorded a $2.5M loss.

In my view, this segment is highly sensitive to food prices, and the company might benefit from focusing on the real estate business instead of exploiting the land itself, especially given the recent results.

On the other hand, where I believe the company could benefit from its expertise is in the loan segment, which delivered a 72% increase in interest income during Q1.

What that means is we have shifted some of our investment to making a few more loans as opposed to property purchases. Because the loans, of course, give us quite a bit higher current yield than owning a farm.

We don't get depreciation, of course, but we do get a higher current yield and it's helping us maintain our cash flow.

Source: Paul Pittman (Executive Chairman), Q1 2025 Earnings Call.

All things considered, and based on the most recent guidance update ($13.3M to $15.9M in AFFO), I expect the company to deliver ~$10M in income to its shareholders (AFFO excluding stock-based compensation and dividends to preferred units).

Considering FPI's $510M market cap, it is clear that the value of the company is not determined by its day-to-day operations, but by the value of its assets.

Discount to NAV

So, how much is Farmland Partners worth?

Since the company generates minimal income, I believe we can approximate its fair value by the market value of its assets less debt and minority interests.

As of the end of the most recent quarter, FPI had $810M in assets, of which $745M is the value of its land, and $218M in liabilities, giving us an equity value of around $592M, which is higher than the current market cap.

However, the current share count stands at 47.1M shares, but after February 2026, the Series A preferred units holders can decide to convert them into common equity, which at current prices would increase the share count by 8.8M shares.

The $592M in equity value divided by the diluted share count gives us a NAV of $10.6 per share, which is not compelling considering that FPI's stock is currently trading at $11.3.

Nonetheless, the value of FPI's land has increased in price since it was first recorded in the balance sheet, so we should take into account the market value.

During Q1 2025, the company sold two farms for a total consideration of $10M, of which $0.8M was recognized as a gain, and during 2024, it sold 54 properties for $312M, of which $54.1M were gains. Based on these figures, we can approximate that the market value of FPI's land is between 10% to 20% higher than the book value (~$856M).

If we calculate the value based on the price per acre, we arrive at a similar figure.

The management stated during the last earnings call that on the highest quality farms, they can get up to $18,000 per acre, but based on the recent sales, the company sold 1.1k acres in Q1 at an average price of $9,000 per acre. At that price, the current 92.4k acres would be worth $840M.

If we assume the market value of FPI's land is $840M, the net asset value per share increases to $12.37.

Conclusion: Not Enough Discount to Fair Value

Although the management has recently stated that the NAV per share stands above $14, this is true if we take into account the current share count.

But when we factor in the share dilution, the stock presents a discount of less than 10% to its NAV. In addition, the company would need to sell these assets, so we should also need to discount the proceeds to the present value.

Considering that I do not expect Farmland Partners to increase its revenues significantly in the short term, and the discount to NAV is not large enough, I believe there might be better investment opportunities.

While I would consider Farmland Partners attractive below $10 per share, at the current prices, I view it as a hold.

data.title: TSMC: The Bull Run Has Only Just Begun
data.description:
Since my last coverage on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM), when I rated this stock as a Strong Buy, shares have appreciated by 57%.

Today's pop after decent Q2 earnings results is a clear sign of the continuation of the bull run that began (or should I say, resumed) after the tariff drama was done in April.

The demand for AI and HPC chips is intact. Furthermore, the high utilization rates for the 5nm process node is among the main drivers for the margin beat. That said, there is a caveat. TSMC beat its own guidance, but didn't beat the Street's consensus for revenue, falling short by $1.23 billion. I believe the spectacular margins (both operating and net profit margins), coupled with a record quarter in terms of profits, overshadowed the top line miss.

Furthermore, management has been conservative (compared to the Street's consensus) for the FY 2025 revenue guidance, projecting a 30% growth vs the Street's 40% expectation. This caution is also reflected in the guidance for operating margins for Q3, where there is a decline compared to Q2, possibly due to the impact of tariffs (both sectoral and reciprocal). I discuss in more detail below the risks to my bull case, although I advance in this introduction that I remain ultra-bullish on this stock, reaffirming my strong buy rating.

While I don't own TSMC directly, I own SOXL, which has TSM among its holdings, so I am indirectly exposed to TSMC through this 4x leveraged ETP.

Earnings Review

Let's start with dessert.

Due to the high demand for AI and HPC chips, along with a higher utilization rate of their 3nm and 5nm process nodes, the company surpassed its revenue and operating margin guidance.

That said, TSMC missed the Street's top line estimate by over $1 billion. The fact that the stock didn't sell off due to this miss is extremely bullish to me. I believe the main reason had to do with the guidance for the next quarter ($32.4 billion at the mid point) being above the consensus, along with an impressive 60% jump in Q2 profit.

I'll be as direct as I can. The main reason I am bullish on TSMC is that I haven't seen a semiconductor manufacturing company bringing in $30 billion per quarter while growing revenue in the mid-30s. Even more impressive is their operating margin of 50%, pretty much the same as Nvidia (NVDA).

Moving on, the 5nm process node is still the most used. In fact, the high capacity utilization of this node was the main driver to offset the margin headwinds due to an unfavorable foreign exchange rate.

In fact, the demand for the 5nm process node is so high that the company was using N7 capacity to support the 5nm process node. That's music to my ears, as the 7nm process node is often used for smartphone applications, a platform that I don't find particularly exciting after looking at the QoQ growth rates below.

In regard the transition to the 3nm node, it is still far away, with management estimating approximately 2 years.

The demand is high because of a lot of AI products still in the 4-nanometer technology node, and they will transition to 3-nanometer probably in next 2 years.

On the negative side, I have to say that I am not particularly excited with the operating margins guided for Q3. Management expects a range between 45.5% and 47.5%, which is below the 49.6% in Q2. I believe management was cautious with this estimate due to the continuation of the tariff drama in the U.S. (both reciprocal and sectoral tariffs on semis), which brings me to my next point.

A (Political) Investment In The U.S.

In March 2025, TSMC announced a $100 billion expansion plan in the U.S. This comes on top of the $65 billion already allocated to the Arizona fabs. The fact that management reaffirmed the $165B investment in the U.S. during the Q2 earnings call is a positive factor from a sectoral and reciprocal tariff perspective.

This expansion includes plans for 6 advanced wafer manufacturing fab in Arizona, 2 advanced packaging fabs and a major R&D center to support the strong multiyear demand from our customers. - C.C. Wei Chairman & CEO Q2 2025 earnings call.

Let me explain.

Since President Trump's threats in April to set a sectoral tariff on semiconductors, starting at 25%, there has been an eerie silence on this topic. However, earlier this week, Trump threatened again with tariffs on semiconductors, which could be implemented as soon as August 1. That's two weeks from now.

What do I think about these tariffs? And, most importantly, is it time to take some profits on my SOXL position (up 161% at the moment of writing this article)?

Not at all. In fact, I am planning to add more in the next days. Here is why.

Trump already hinted at his plans during the announcement this week (emphasis added).

probably at the end of the month, and we’re going to start off with a low tariff and give the pharmaceutical companies a year or so to build, and then we’re going to make it a very high tariff.

Does this sound to you like another tariff pause? It does to me. While in his statement, he referred to tariffs on pharmaceuticals, I believe tariffs on semis will follow a similar fate. In fact, I anticipate (with a high level of certainty) that around August 1, we will see an announcement from the White House demanding a 25% tariff on semiconductors... starting a year from now. I believe the rate until then should be mid to high single digits, 10% in the worst case scenario.

As for the 32% reciprocal tariff with Taiwan set on Liberation Day, I believe TSMC's $165 billion investment in the U.S. will help the Taiwanese government to negotiate a significantly lower rate. I project (with a high level of certainty) that the rate will stay at 10% after the August 1 deadline.

Overall, I anticipate a total tariff rate between 15%-20% on semiconductors after August 1.

That's not the best news for TSMC, however, I believe they will easily pass a good part of these costs to U.S. customers, in the likes of Nvidia, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), or Intel (INTC).

I believe management does expect a similar tariff value as well, as noted in one of the comments during the earnings call (emphasis added).

We take into the consideration of the possible impact of tariff and a lot of other uncertainties. So we become more conservative. That's our current attitude.

And indeed, management has been quite conservative with their estimates. The company expects revenue for FY 2025 to grow around 30% in U.S. dollar terms vs. the Street's consensus of 40%. In fact, during the earnings call, one of the analysts asked whether Nvidia's resumption of H20 shipments to China could be a tailwind to get to a growth rate in the mid 40s. Here is CEO Wei's reply (emphasis added):

I mean that's -- China is a big market and my customer can still continue to supply the chip to the big market. And it's a very positive news for them. And in return, it's a very positive news to TSMC. Whether we are ready to increase our forecast, not yet. Another quarter probably will be more appropriate to answer your question.

There you go. I believe management bought themselves that extra quarter for the FY 2025 guidance, not primarily to monitor Nvidia’s performance in China but to gain more clarity on where tariff rates will settle come August 1.

Looking Ahead

I'd keep a close eye on where the total tariffs (reciprocal + sectoral) land after August 1. As I said in the previous section, I believe the effective rate will be between 15-20%, which may already be priced into management's guidance for both revenue and, most importantly, profit margins.

The fact that TSMC expects a drop in operating margins in Q3 is an indication that they expect some impact from these tariffs.

While I believe TSMC will be able to pass most of the extra costs to U.S. players in the likes of Nvidia, AMD, and Intel, if tariffs come in at a rate of 32% reciprocal and 25% sectoral, then my bull case may be at risk, as the company may have to absorb a good amount of the additional costs, despite the high demand for AI and HPC chips in the U.S. That could also depress sales and lead to a panic selloff, similar to the one we have seen in Q1 this year.

Overall, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating, but I caution that volatility around August 1 is not completely off the charts.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
