data.title: Uber: The 'Netflix Of Mobility' Set To Gain Big From The Autonomous Vehicle Race
data.description:
Despite reaching recent market highs, Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) is a compelling long-term investment opportunity as a profitable, cash-generating, global blue-chip platform that will benefit from secular trends in transportation and online delivery.

Its entrenched and leadership market position in transport and food-delivery enables Uber to leverage its scale and improve its already profitable asset-light business model. Different global partnerships with autonomous vehicle companies (AV), both in the US and internationally, make it a standout in the mobility space. As (Netflix) or (Amazon) have dominated the streaming and e-commerce business, similar reasons are also driving Uber's success.

For years, investors have feared that regulatory risks tied to the gig economy could undermine Uber’s business model, especially as courts and lawmakers increasingly push to classify drivers as employees rather than independent contractors. The rise of AVs could render this debate obsolete. More importantly, AVs should improve unit economics and expand the addressable market for ride-hailing, as car ownership declines and consumers increasingly turn to shared mobility. In that context, Uber stands to benefit from higher efficiency, lower costs, and broader adoption.

While Uber trades at a premium compared to peers (around 20% if you see EV/EBITDA), the market is pricing in over 30% revenue growth an´d a substantial improvement in EBITDA margin, from the current 10% to 30%. While these outcomes are possible, they appear to some level priced in at current levels. I recommend considering entry below $90-100 or selling puts to generate income and potentially acquire shares at a discount. For patient investors, I wouldn't mind buying at these levels given the long-term upside.

Global Brand with Network Effects and Duopoly Dynamics

Uber has become a verb in a market with limited differentiation. Its ride-hailing business operates under one of the most recognized and trusted transportation brands globally, with a presence in over 70 countries, where it leads in most of them. In the US, Uber and Lyft effectively function as a duopoly in the ride-sharing space. In other major markets for Uber, as the UK, Mexico, Canada, Brazil, and Australia, Uber holds a dominant position, often capturing over 60–70% share.

While the company once faced intense competition, the industry has seen significant consolidation. Uber exited markets with strong local rivals, such as China, India, and several Southeast Asian countries, by selling its operations to competitors like Grab. In the remaining markets, Uber has solidified its leadership position.

This scale-driven dominance provides durable pricing power and reduces customer churn. Moreover, Uber benefits from increased asset utilization (very relevant for AI players) and operating leverage as its network grows. Regarding the delivery business, although Uber is the second-largest player in the U.S., it still benefits from significant network effects and the Uber One loyalty program. While profitability remains a challenge for grocery delivery, speed and efficiency are increasingly important. This is why companies like Instacart have outperformed players like FreshDirect. Uber’s extensive network positions it well to compete and potentially win in this space.

AV: Business Model and Profitability Boost

I envision Uber becoming the Netflix of transportation, the leading platform that aggregates and distributes services, while enabling other players in the ecosystem to operate through it. In the same way, Netflix emerged as the dominant force in the streaming industry by controlling the customer interface while working with studios, content creators, producers, and actors, Uber is positioning itself similarly in mobility and delivery.

While Uber and Lyft operate in the referred duopoly in the US, the AV space is far more fragmented, with numerous players such as Waymo, (MBLY), May Mobility, and various Chinese companies all vying for their space.

We’ve seen similar dynamics in other industries. In retail logistics, asset-light platforms like (CART), (DOOR), and (AMZN) have outperformed asset-heavy models like Ocado or FreshDirect. Ultimately, what matters most is control over the customer. Achieving this requires significant investment in network effects, something Uber has already done at scale. Recently, smaller competitors like InDrive have attempted unsuccessfully to enter US market.

As illustrated in the graph below provided by Lyft, the Robotaxi value-chain is broad and involves very different activities and tasks. Balancing demand, managing fleet, etc, are different businesses from constructing AV. Uber, through its platform, holds a powerful position between self-driving technology and end consumers.

Very important to that, is how AV is a changing catalyst for Uber’s unit economics is the deployment of AVs. Driver compensation accounts for around 75% of an Uber ride. By removing the human driver, gross margins should expand substantially, improving profitability per ride. Legal and regulatory risks will decline as AVs reduce labor classification disputes. Efficiency and asset utilization will increase, especially with 24/7 robotaxi availability. AV providers compete to be on Uber’s platform, ensuring Uber maintains pricing power and consumer access, without the need for full vertical integration.

The fact that Uber is having partnerships with most AV companies is a proof of that. Also, for robotaxis to recover investments, full utilization of AV cars is necessary and probably only Uber is capable of doing that in most markets. For 2025 and 2026, aside from the launches in many US cities, Uber has mentioned it will be deploying robotaxis in Tokyo, Abu Dhabi, etc.

Finally, as has been noted repeatedly, it will likely take many years for autonomous vehicles (AVs) to become mainstream in the U.S. The technology still faces significant challenges, particularly in adverse weather conditions like rain and snow.In the meantime, ride-sharing platforms will continue to grow more rapidly, benefiting from greater operating leverage as adoption increases. In the US, regulatory progress remains slow and fragmented, often handled at the state level, which adds to the timeline, as even Elon Musk has pointed out in his public frustrations.

And while international expansion may seem appealing, keep in mind that dense traffic and infrastructure challenges abroad may further delay AV adoption in many global cities.

From Growth to Durable Profitability

Uber has successfully transitioned from a cash-burning disruptor to a mature, blue-chip technology platform. The company is now consistently profitable on a GAAP basis, generating positive free cash flow, and has demonstrated strong cost discipline and capital efficiency. This shift has validated its platform model and de-risked the long-term investment case. It is also in the moment where it starts to gain cash from leverage, with operating income and EBITDA growing over 100% YoY. Other revenue streams as advertisements, should help improve unit economics in the same way they helped Inscart become profitable.

On a financial perspective, with nearly half of its revenue generated outside the U.S., Uber stands to benefit from a weaker U.S. dollar, which will inflate the dollar-reported value of international revenue and cash flow.

Finally, there are promising opportunities in emerging segments such as Uber One, loyalty programs, online groceries, alcohol, and pharmacy deliveries. While Uber may have some regrets about the Cornershop acquisition due to integration challenges, the market still holds significant long-term potential. Finally, AVs could play a key role in making the delivery business more profitable. Compared to ride-sharing, the delivery business has always faced tougher unit economics.

Valuation

Uber trades at a premium compared to its peers, particularly Lyft, which, in my view, represents a high-conviction investment and a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. However, I believe the premium is justified considering its global presence, dominant position in most markets, and strong brand

Also, Uber is executing a $4 Bn share buy-back which should improve the P/E and EV / EBITDA as the number of shares is reduced. This programe represents around 4% of Uber´s market capitalization which should also provide some support.

Based on the table below, the EV/EBITDA multiple of 22x appears quite stretched. This valuation implies an expected growth rate of over 50%. While aggressive, such growth is possible, as operating leverage takes effect. The current 22X multiple also assumes EBITDA margins will rise to around 30%, which may seem ambitious but could be attainable under leverage, AV, the right execution.

I run a DCF considering $80 Bn of revenues by 2029, a 9% WACC, 4% of terminal growth, and an EBITDA margin expansion, from the current 10-12% to 30%, driven by operating leverage.

The FCF terminal value considered is the last three years average to make our forecasts more conservative. As you can see, the model yields a valuation above $100 per share.

Risks

I believe the main risk is that Uber's stock has already seen significant growth, making the current valuation somewhat stretched. For patient investors, I wouldn't mind holding or even buying at current levels, but some caution is warranted.

That said, despite my earlier comments on autonomous vehicles (AVs), there's still a competitive threat, particularly from Waymo, which benefits from the deep financial backing of Alphabet. The latter could decide to burn cash for the very long term and hinder Uber´s position.

Final remarks

Uber has evolved into a tech-enabled transportation utility, globally integrated, capital-light, and increasingly automated. Its ongoing profitability improvement, combined with potential upside from AV, presents a strong case for margin expansion and sustained long-term earnings growth.

With its market dominance and widespread app adoption, Uber is well-positioned to expand (and further) into adjacent verticals such as fintech (e.g., driver lending), advertising, and other high-margin opportunities.

Given these dynamics, Uber stands out as a compelling long-term core holding for a forward-looking equity portfolio. Over the next 5 to 10 years, I believe it has the potential to become a trillion-dollar market capitalization company in a decade, which is multiplying by 5 its current $200 Bn value.

data.title: Spotify Technology Is A Winner, But Not A Buy At This High Price
data.description:
Why I’m Hitting Pause on Spotify’s Stock

Here’s where I’m at with Spotify Technology (NYSE: NYSE:SPOT): they’ve made the switch from a high-growth cash burner to a business that actually makes money—and that’s no small feat. Profitability isn’t just a headline; it’s starting to show up consistently and that alone is a huge deal if you’ve followed their story for a while. The catch is, the market has caught on. Over the past year, the stock has exploded, more than doubling and it’s up over 40% just since the last time I looked at it. At $705 a share, I think most of the easy upside has been made.

If I had to sum up my stance, I’m saying Hold—not because I’m suddenly negative but because the trade-off isn’t as good as before. All the good news is out in the open now. To move higher from here, Spotify will need to hit nearly every target they set, or make a big difference with a surprise. There’s not much room for mistakes when expectations are this high.

How Spotify Keeps Leading the Pack

Spotify is still the king of streaming music and audio, for sure. With 678 million users around the world, they’re the platform everyone thinks of when they hear “streaming music.” What stands out lately is how they’ve gone beyond music—bringing in podcasts and more recently, audiobooks. They’re not just talking about being a “platform”; they’re making it happen.

The company’s financial results support this story. This past quarter, Spotify pulled in €4.19 billion in revenue, which is up 15% from last year. They managed to control their spending on research and development and sales and marketing, even while growing. Net income landed at €225 million and gross profit was the highest it’s ever been. That’s not just luck; that’s just good management.

Let’s not forget about the business moves—they’re always looking for new ways to grow. Rumors of a Netflix tie-up are just the latest example of how they’re thinking about the future, not just the next quarter.

The Streaming World: More Crowded Than Ever

Audio streaming as a whole is still growing but there’s a lot more competition than there used to be. Podcasts are a must-have and AI-powered playlists and personalized content are quickly becoming the standard. Spotify is still ahead when it comes to users and time spent on the platform but Apple Music, Amazon, YouTube and even smaller companies are not sitting still.

Regulation is another big topic, especially in Europe where new rules about how apps can charge users could change how much money Spotify gets to keep from every subscription. That might help them, or it might just make things more complicated. Either way, regulatory shifts are something to watch.

What the Numbers Really Say

The story is in the numbers. In the latest quarter, Spotify’s revenue hit €4.19 billion, up 15%. Gross profit reached €1.33 billion, with a margin above 31%, which is better than it’s ever been. Operating income was €509 million, miles ahead of last year. Free cash flow remains strong and they’re sitting on €7 billion in cash—plenty of money for whatever comes next. The company has relatively little debt, a healthy return on equity and they’re now funding their own growth instead of needing investors to give them more money.

One thing that stands out is that margins are finally looking solid and the days of “profit someday” are gone. Spotify is delivering real earnings, not just to grow.

Is Spotify’s Price Too High?

Now, about the price. This is where things get harder. Spotify trades at a forward P/E ratio of about 72, with EV/EBITDA over 70 and price-to-book around 22. Even compared to other high-growth tech companies, that’s a steep valuation. You have to believe that the company will keep growing at double digits and keep finding ways to improve margins for the next several years to justify paying this much.

If you run the math on what analysts expect—almost $19 in earnings per share by 2027—and even use a generous multiple, the fair value you come up with is pretty much right where the stock trades today. There’s just not a big gap between where Spotify is and where the numbers say it could go, unless something changes.

Compared to peers, the stock looks expensive on almost every measure. It’s more expensive than Netflix on an earnings basis and it trades at a big premium to traditional media and digital companies. You can make the case that it deserves some of that premium but at this level, it seems too high.

What the Headlines and Analysts Are Saying

The talk about Spotify has been mostly positive. Analyst sentiment still says they are doing well in streaming audio and there’s a lot of excitement about what they might do with partnerships or new technology. Headlines show plenty of optimism but it’s clear that expectations are higher than ever and the market is watching closely to see if Spotify can keep delivering real progress—not just big promises.

Where Things Could Go Wrong

Here’s where I’m keeping my guard up. The main risk is simple: valuation. At these prices, if they fall short or slow down—on revenue, profit, or even guidance—it could send the stock much lower. Competitors like Apple and Amazon can afford to compete on price for as long as they want, which could force Spotify to spend more or cut prices. The cost of music rights isn’t going down either and negotiations with big record labels are always hard. If ad spending takes a hit in a weak economy, Spotify’s ad-supported business will feel the pain. Regulatory issues are also there, especially in Europe. Even with the best management team and execution, things can change for the worse.

On the bright side, the company’s move into podcasts and audiobooks, its strong brand and the fact that it’s sitting on so much cash help protect against these risks. They have options.

What to Watch Next

What happens next? The next couple of quarters are going to be important. Investors are watching to see if Spotify can keep growing margins and getting more money from each user. Over the long haul, the expectation is still for steady, strong growth and higher profits over time, as more people pay for premium and the company finds new ways to monetize content.

If Spotify can deliver a big surprise—a new business, a partnership, or a new technology that stands out—there could be more upside. But after a 136% rally in a year, I think it’s fair to say expectations are very high.

How the Future Could Play Out

In the worst case, a global recession, a price war, or a failed negotiation with record labels could cause revenue growth to slow and margins to shrink, sending the stock back toward $400–$500. In the “base case,” Spotify hits consensus: steady growth, a bit more margin expansion and decent cash flow. The stock probably trades sideways or creeps a little higher, maybe into the $650–$725 range. The bull case would take a big win: big price hikes that stick, a major leap forward in podcasts or audiobooks, or a killer new product that catches fire. Then and only then, do I see the stock making a run at $900 or more—but to be honest, that scenario doesn’t feel far from what’s already included in the current price.

My Final Take

To wrap this up, I still believe Spotify is a fantastic company and an industry leader. But at $705 a share, with all the great news and big expectations already priced in, I don’t see the same opportunity I saw before. If you already own it, I’d hang on and see how the next few quarters turn out. If you’re thinking about buying, I’d wait for a pullback—something in the $600–$625 range would get my attention, since that would bring Spotify’s valuation down to a more reasonable level and offer a better margin of safety if growth slows or the market stumbles, especially if the setback isn't tied to fundamentals. At this stage, patience pays. Let Spotify catch its breath and see if another leg higher is really in the cards.
